text: Tensile, creep, and relaxation tests were performed on INOR-8, a nickel-base alloy developed for use in the Molten-Salt Reactor.
The chemical, metallurgical, and nuclear properties required of a structural material for use in the Molten-Salt Reactor brought about the development of a nickel-base alloy, designated INOR-8.
(ref 1) As soon as commercial heats of this material became available, mechanical properties studies were initiated by several laboratories.

The composition specified for INOR-8 is also given and a comparison of the analyses reveals that the most significant variation occurs in the carbon content which ranges from $0.02\%$ for SP 16 to $0.14\%$ for 8M-1 and 1327.
Only SP 19 and M-1566 are within the present carbon specifications.
For most heats this treatment was for 1 hr at $2100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Heat SP 16 developed a coarse-grain size under these conditions, however, and the temperature was reduced to $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for most of the test series on this heat.

The sheet specimens were 0.063-in. thick, 0.5-in. wide, with a 3-in. uniform gage length.
Rod specimens were of the standard ASTM design for 0.505- or 0.357-in.-diam gage sections.
Tests were performed on a Baldwin-Southwark hydraulic testing machine having a 120,000-lb capacity.
In all cases the extension rate was 0.05 in. per min.
Typical Data: A series of tensile curves for INOR-8 rod specimens (SP 16 annealed at $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) is shown in Fig.

The stress at the proportional limit and the $0.2\%$ offset yield strength (indicated by the dash on the tensile curve) exhibit very little temperature dependence between 1000 and $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Rods Annealed 1 hr at $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Effect of Composition and Grain Size: The tensile properties for sheet specimens of four heats-SP 16, SP 19, M-1566, and 8M-1 (data for SP 19 and 8M-1 are for 0.045-in.-thick sheet and were taken from a test series performed for Inouye) are presented in Figs.
8 indicate that the tensile strengths do not vary greatly from heat to heat.
Figure 9 shows the variation in the yield strength from heat to heat.
Heat 8M-1, high in carbon with a fine-grain size, exhibits the highest yield strength; while SP 16, low in carbon and coarse grained, is the weakest.
Values range from 25,000 psi to 38,000 psi at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The elongation is constant up to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , but rapidly drops to a minimum value near $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the highest temperature investigated.
The elongation decreases with increasing carbon content and/or decreasing grain size with the exception of M-1566.
Heat M-1566 is the least ductile above $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A summary of these and additional tensile data are given in Tables A-1 and A-2 in the Appendix.
Effect of Notches: Tests were performed on notched-rod specimens of SP 19-3 at several temperatures.
These specimens had a gage diameter of 0.357 in. and a notch radius of 0.005 in.
As in the case of most metals, the effect of the notch is to increase the ultimate tensile strength; but at the lower temperatures, the increase for INOR-8 is only slight.
The notched to unnotched strength ratios at room temperature, 1000, 1200, and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are 1.08, 1.07, 1.13, and 1.38, respectively.
Data for these tests are presented in Table A-3 of the Appendix.
Effect of Aging: A few aging tests were performed on notched specimens of SP 19-3.
The selected treatments were 200 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , 40 hr at $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and 4 hr at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Data are summarized in Table A-3 of the Appendix.
The results do not indicate any significant aging effect, although the notch strength ratios are very close to unity below $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Data are also reported in Table A-3 of the Appendix for smooth rods of coarse-grained SP 19-1 aged 40 hr at $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
No change in the strength properties is evident, but the elongation at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ has increased from 20 to $50\%$ .

Effect of Carburization: A few tensile tests were performed on smooth- and notched-rod specimens of SP 19-3 which had been carburized in sodium-graphite for $40\mathrm{hr}$ at $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
This treatment resulted in a high-carbon case which penetrated to a depth of about 0.010 in.
Up to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ carburization results in a slight increase in the yield strength and a decrease in the tensile strength, elongation, and reduction in area.
Summary data are reported in Table A-3 of the Appendix.

The bulk of the testing was done on heats SP 16 and SP 19, but a few tests were performed on the other three heats for comparative purposes.

section: Equipment and Procedure for Tests in Salt
text: The testing chambers were constructed of Inconel, Hastelloy B, or Hastelloy C and are described together with other equipment in a report written by Douglas and Manly.<sup>7</sup>

section: Equipment and Procedure for Tests in Salt
text: Such a technique lead to scatter and inaccuracies in the strain measurements especially for strains less than $0.5\%$ .
Typical Data: Typical creep curves for tests in salt at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are shown in Fig.
(Unless otherwise stated, SP 16 has been given a 1-hr anneal at $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .) Creep occurs in the three classical stages: transient, steady, and accelerating.
Most tests in salt exhibited this type of curve, although many of the low stress tests on SP 16 at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and above exhibited a continually decreasing creep rate to rupture.
Figure 17 is a comparison of the creep curves for the five heats at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 20,000 psi.
With the exception of heat M-1566, the curves show fairly good agreement.
Heat 8M-1 exhibits the lowest creep rate while heat 1327 is the most ductile.
Heat M-1566 exhibits an inflection at the end of transient creep and accelerates immediately to rupture after a short time and small strain.
17 Comparison of the Creep Curves for Various Heats of INOR-8 Tested in Molten Salt at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 20,000 psi.
Summary Data: A digest of the creep data obtained from tests in molten salt is presented in Table A-4 of the Appendix.
Figure 18 is a log-log plot of the stress vs time to $1\%$ creep strain.
At 1100, 1200, and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ these bands are nearly parallel, while the slopes at 1650 and $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are apparently different.
Air test data indicate a break in the curves above 20,000 psi, near the yield strength, but the data obtained from tests in molten salt exhibit too much scatter to define this break clearly.
Figure 19 is a log-log plot of the stress vs the minimum creep rate.

18, except that: (1) the scatterband for $1100^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ data is not parallel to that at 1200 and $1300^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , and (2) the data at $1500^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ and above show considerably greater stress dependency.
Figure 20 is a log-log plot of the stress vs the rupture life.
Scatterbands resemble those for $1\%$ creep at 1100, 1200, and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , but here again a considerable stress dependency occurs at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and above.
The rupture ductility values recorded for INOR-8 are lowest at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The minimum value listed in Table A-4 of the Appendix is $1.7\%$ which corresponds to 12,725 hr at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The maximum value reported is $22\%$ for heat 1327 at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ after 23 hr.
2l through 25 for the various heats of INOR-8 tested in molten salt at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and 20,000 psi.
17.) Fracture occurs by intergranular cracking, but the grain size apparently has not affected the rupture life at this temperature.
Heat 1327, for example, exhibits a structure and crack pattern similar to heat M-1566, yet one lasted 1177 hr and the other only 180 hr.
Heat SP 16, which is coarse grained as compared to heats 1327 and M-1566, failed after 882 hr, a time between the two extremes.

text: The creep program in air consisted of a series of tests on rod and sheet specimens of SP 16 at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and on sheet specimens of SP 19 at several temperatures.

text: 41 The Effect of Carburization on the Creep Curve for INOR-8 (SP 16) at 20,000 psi and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Relaxation tests were performed on 0.357-in.-diam rod specimens of SP 16, with equipment described by Kennedy and Douglas.[9] The tests were performed in air over a temperature range from 1150 to $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Extensions between 0.05 and $0.2\%$ were employed, although most tests were conducted at 0.05 or $0.1\%$ .
These curves show selected data at 1200, 1300, 1400, 1500, and $1600^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ for extensions of 0.05 and $0.1\%$ .
Additional data are provided in Table A-7 of the Appendix.
The curves at 1200 and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ reveal an interesting phenomenon which was common in relaxation testing of INOR-8.
Near $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ it appears to last between 10 and 50 hr, while above $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ it is present only for a fraction of an hour.
At 1100 and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ there are indications that the creep strength improves with increasing carbon and decreasing grain size.
At $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , however, heat M-1566, which has an "average" composition, is the weakest while the high-carbon and fine-grained heats are not significantly stronger than SP 16.
Above $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ further variations arise which cannot be clarified without additional data.
It is possible, of course, that $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is a transition temperature above which the coarse-grained SP 16 is stronger than the fine-grained heats.
The very slight contraction, sometimes observed in SP 16 during the initial period of creep or relaxation tests, might be explained by assuming a volume contraction associated with precipitation.

Furthermore, the continually decreasing strain rates in SP 16 for low stresses at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and above might be explained by the strengthening produced through the precipitation and coarsening of the carbides along the grain boundaries.
Attempts have been made to establish a time-temperature correlation for creep and rupture properties of INOR-8.
Haynes-Stellite presents Larson-Miller plots but a correlation can also be obtained for the Dorn-Shepard Parameter.[11] The activation energy for creep, relaxation, and
recrystallization were calculated to be near 83,200 cal/mole-°K.
This is a plot of the Dorn-Shepard parameter for $1\%$ creep strain against the log of stress.
Most of the creep data for INOR-8 is included in the plot although the various heats are not distinguished from one another.
The data obtained by the Mechanical Testing Group constitute a major portion of the mechanical property data available on INOR-8.
Significant contributions have been made by other investigators, however, and their results should be considered in evaluating the overall strength properties of INOR-8.
For example, the Haynes-Stellite Company reports stress-rupture, tensile, impact, and creep data for both cast and wrought SP 16.

Inouye<sup>12</sup> has performed aging studies on several heats of INOR-8 for temperatures up to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and times as long as 10,000 hr and the Welding and Brazing Group<sup>13,14,15</sup> has studied the weld metal tensile and bending properties of several heats.
The cold work and recrystallization characteristics of SP 16 have been reported by Spruiell<sup>16</sup> while Carlson<sup>17</sup> has conducted high-temperature fatigue tests on SP 19.
Finally, Cook and Jansen<sup>18</sup> are presently investigating the effect of carburization on the tensile and creep properties of SP 16.
Jansen, A Preliminary Summary of Studies of INOR-8, Inconel, Graphite, and Fluoride System for the MSRP for the Period from May 1, 1958, to Dec.

Since the creep and tensile properties of the stainless steels, with a few exceptions, vary sharply with annealing treatment an actual numerical comparison between INOR-8 and these alloys is not too significant.
Possibly one of the fairest comparisons which could be made would be for types 316 and 304 stainless steel and INOR-8 rod material in air at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The stress to produce a minimum creep rate of $10^{-5}\%/\mathrm{hr}$ (usually stated $0.01\%$ in 1000 hr), a criterion on which high-temperature design stresses are often based, is about 5250 psi for type 316 stainless steel, 3200 for type 304 stainless steel, $^{19}$ and 5350 psi for INOR-8.
If the stress to produce $1\%$ creep in 100,000 hr were selected from the salt data, however, the value for INOR-8 would be 4300 psi.
This figure is still above the design stress for type 304 stainless steel.
Fractions of the minimum observed yield strength and tensile strength could also be obtained in accordance with the Unfired Pressure Vessel Code. $^{20}$ Such data have been plotted in Fig.
45 and compared with design stresses for type 316 stainless steel.

The tensile ductility up to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ appears to be satisfactory for wrought, cast, and weld metal, but Cook and Jansen<sup>18</sup> report a figure of only $7.75\%$ for SP 16 carburized at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and tested at room temperature.
The poorest creep ductilities occur at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , but are still above $1\%$ even after more than 12,000 hr of exposure.
The results of this investigation reveal the range of tensile and creep properties which INOR-8 can be expected to exhibit when the composition and grain size are permitted to vary significantly.

The short-time data, however, reveal that at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the fine-grained material is slightly stronger while at temperatures above $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ a coarse-grain size is desirable.
The long-time creep properties of coarse-grained INOR-8 are better than many of the stainless steels.
Certain heats exhibit low values, especially at temperatures above $1300^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ for tensile tests and around $1100^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ in creep tests.
